events.text: The patient is a 58-year-old Hispanic woman named Marisol Fuentes-Delgado. She has a significant family history of breast and ovarian cancer (mother with breast cancer at age 42, maternal aunt with ovarian cancer). She is a former smoker (10 pack-years, quit 15 years prior), with a medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (on metformin), hypertension (on lisinopril), and obesity (BMI 33.2). She is postmenopausal. ECOG performance status 1 at time of diagnosis.

At age 58 years, the patient had a diagnosis of stage IIB (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition) CARCINOMA, DUCTAL, INFILTRATING (ICD-O histology code 8500/3) of the LEFT BREAST, UPPER OUTER QUADRANT (ICD-O site code C50.4). Relevant biomarkers included: Estrogen Receptor (ER): Negative (0% staining, Allred score 0); Progesterone Receptor (PR): Negative (0% staining, Allred score 0); HER2 by IHC: Negative (1+); HER2 by FISH: Not amplified (HER2/CEP17 ratio 1.2); Ki-67: 92%; BRCA1/2 germline testing: BRCA1 pathogenic variant detected (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74); PD-L1 CPS (22C3 assay): 28 (positive). Tumor grade: Histologic grade 3 (Nottingham grade: tubular formation 3, nuclear pleomorphism 3, mitotic count 3). Tumor size: 3.4 cm by ultrasound. Lymph nodes: 1 clinically suspicious axillary lymph node on imaging confirmed by ultrasound-guided FNA. Bilateral breasts examined; no contralateral lesion. Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) subtype, basal-like. Other relevant diagnostic information: Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via core needle biopsy; Laterality: Left; Lymphovascular invasion: Present; Androgen Receptor (AR): Negative (<1%); Germline genetic counseling completed prior to treatment initiation.

At age 58 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY (core needle biopsy, left breast, 12 o'clock position, ultrasound-guided). The specimen showed invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3 (Nottingham grade 9/9). Tumor cells were arranged in solid sheets and nests with central necrosis. Nuclei were markedly pleomorphic with prominent nucleoli. Mitotic rate was 28 per 10 HPF. No in-situ component identified on this core biopsy specimen. Immunohistochemistry: ER negative (0%, Allred 0), PR negative (0%, Allred 0), HER2 1+ (IHC), Ki-67 92%, CK5/6 positive (basal-like phenotype), EGFR positive (2+), p53 overexpression (strong diffuse, suggesting TP53 mutation), E-cadherin intact, AR negative. PD-L1 CPS 28 (22C3 assay). Pathologic diagnosis: Invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, triple-negative, basal-like subtype.

At age 58 years, the patient had a DIAGNOSTIC MAMMOGRAM AND BREAST ULTRASOUND (bilateral), which showed: Left breast - a 3.4 x 2.9 x 3.1 cm irregular, hyperdense mass at 12 o'clock position, 4 cm from nipple, with associated skin thickening but no skin invasion. Left axilla - one enlarged lymph node (1.8 cm, cortical thickening 8 mm, loss of fatty hilum) suspicious for metastatic involvement. Right breast - no suspicious lesion identified. ACR BI-RADS 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy). Cancer status: Present. Metastatic sites: Left axillary lymph node suspicious.

At age 58 years, the patient had a STAGING CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST, which showed: No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Lungs: No pulmonary nodules or masses. Mediastinum: No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Liver: No hepatic lesions. Abdomen/pelvis: No suspicious lymphadenopathy or organ metastases. Left axilla: Enlarged left axillary lymph node as noted on prior ultrasound. Bones: No lytic or blastic lesions identified. Impression: No distant metastases. Consistent with locoregional disease only. Cancer status: Locoregional disease, no distant metastases.

At age 58 years, the patient had a BONE SCAN (technetium-99m), which showed: No evidence of osseous metastatic disease. Mild degenerative changes in the lumbar spine and bilateral knees (bilateral knee osteoarthritis), consistent with degenerative etiology. Cancer status: No osseous metastases.

At age 58 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for triple-negative breast cancer based on a specimen obtained from the primary left breast tumor (core needle biopsy). A 500-gene panel was used (FoundationOne CDx). Clinically actionable alterations: TP53 p.R248W (hotspot mutation, variant allele frequency 68%, pathogenic); BRCA1 c.5266dupC germline pathogenic variant confirmed in tumor (loss of heterozygosity at BRCA1 locus, consistent with biallelic inactivation); PIK3CA wild-type; PTEN loss (homozygous deletion); RB1 p.L153Rfs*11 (frameshift, loss of function); CDKN2A p.P114L (pathogenic); MYC amplification (copy number 18.4, high-level amplification). Additional somatic mutations (VUS or likely benign): NF1 p.D1644N (VUS); ARID1A p.Q330* (truncating, likely pathogenic); FAT3 p.S2152L (VUS); SPEN p.R2196H (VUS); RNF43 p.G659Vfs*41 (truncating, VUS); KDM6A p.R479C (VUS); ZFHX3 p.D1636N (VUS). Fusions: No clinically significant gene fusions detected. Copy number alterations: MYC amplification (8q24), PTEN homozygous deletion (10q23), CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion (9p21), RB1 deletion (13q14), MDM4 amplification (1q32). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). Tumor mutational burden: 9 mut/Mb (intermediate). Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score: 62 (high, above threshold of 42). Genomic loss of heterozygosity (LOH): 24% genome-wide LOH. Mutational signature analysis: SBS3 (HRD/BRCA-associated) dominant signature. PD-L1 CPS 28 (from IHC, confirmed). No NTRK fusions detected. No PIK3CA hotspot mutations. No ESR1 mutations.

At age 58 years, the patient received neoadjuvant-intent pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks (cycle 1) concurrent with the initiation of dose-dense doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV plus cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 IV every 2 weeks (ddAC, cycle 1 of 4). Filgrastim was prescribed for growth factor support given dose-dense schedule and patient's age. Metformin was held on days of chemotherapy per institutional protocol. Antiemetic prophylaxis: ondansetron, dexamethasone, and fosaprepitant. Baseline LVEF by echocardiogram: 62%.

At age 58 years (week 3 of neoadjuvant therapy), the patient experienced grade 2 fatigue and grade 2 nausea/vomiting attributed to ddAC chemotherapy. She also developed grade 1 alopecia (total alopecia by cycle 2). Blood counts: ANC nadir of 0.8 x10^9/L (grade 3 neutropenia) after cycle 1 of ddAC, managed with filgrastim dose adjustment and no dose reduction required. Additionally, grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (fingertip tingling) noted, attributed to early taxane sensitization in setting of hyperglycemia; endocrinology consulted for improved glycemic control. No immune-related adverse events from pembrolizumab at this time. HbA1c was 8.4%, metformin dose increased.

At age 58 years (after cycle 2 of ddAC/pembrolizumab, approximately week 6), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Clinical breast exam showed a measurable decrease in palpable tumor size (estimated 2.0 cm vs. 3.4 cm at diagnosis). Left axillary lymph node no longer palpable. Patient tolerating therapy with manageable side effects. ECOG PS 1. Continued on protocol. Cancer present but responding to therapy. No new symptoms. Blood pressure elevated to 148/92 mmHg; lisinopril dose increased. Diabetes management ongoing with endocrinology co-management.

At age 58 years (mid-neoadjuvant, after cycle 4 of ddAC/pembrolizumab), the patient had a BREAST MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST, which showed: Left breast - residual enhancing mass measuring 1.6 x 1.4 cm (down from 3.4 cm at baseline; partial radiographic response, approximately 53% reduction). No new skin or chest wall involvement. Left axilla - previously suspicious lymph node now measures 1.0 cm with partially restored fatty hilum; may represent partial treatment response. No new suspicious lesions in either breast. No distant sites of disease. Cancer status: Present, partial response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Responding but residual disease remains.

At age 58 years (week 10, during transition to taxane phase), the patient developed grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism attributed to pembrolizumab. TSH 18.4 mU/L, free T4 0.6 ng/dL. Endocrinology consulted. Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily initiated. Pembrolizumab continued without dose interruption per KEYNOTE-522 protocol guidelines. The patient also developed grade 2 arthralgia affecting bilateral knees and wrists (likely immune-related arthritis vs. worsening osteoarthritis); NSAIDs initiated (ibuprofen 400 mg TID with food). No elevated liver enzymes, no skin rash, no pneumonitis.

At age 58 years (approximately week 13 of neoadjuvant therapy), the patient transitioned to weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 IV for 12 weeks concurrent with pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks (cycles 5-8 of pembrolizumab). Carboplatin was NOT added to the paclitaxel phase given the patient's borderline renal function (CrCl 52 mL/min by CKD-EPI, attributed to longstanding hypertension and diabetes) and physician discretion to avoid cumulative nephrotoxicity and myelosuppression. Pre-medications included dexamethasone 20 mg, diphenhydramine 50 mg, and famotidine 20 mg prior to each paclitaxel infusion.

At age 58 years (week 18, during weekly paclitaxel/pembrolizumab), the patient developed grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy (loss of sensation in hands and feet to mid-palm and mid-foot level, affecting fine motor tasks and gait). Neurology was consulted. Paclitaxel dose was reduced to 65 mg/m2 (approximately 20% reduction) and duloxetine 30 mg daily was initiated for neuropathic pain management. Patient declined further dose reduction. Grade 2 nail changes (onycholysis of bilateral great toes) also noted. No discontinuation of therapy. Pembrolizumab continued without modification.

At age 58 years (pre-surgery assessment, approximately week 26, after completion of all neoadjuvant therapy), the patient had an oncologist and breast surgery pre-operative office assessment. She completed 8 cycles of pembrolizumab (neoadjuvant phase), 4 cycles of ddAC, and 12 weeks of paclitaxel (dose-reduced for last 4 weeks). Clinical breast exam: small residual palpable firmness at prior tumor site (estimated < 1.5 cm, consistent with fibrosis vs. residual tumor). Left axilla: no palpable lymphadenopathy. ECOG PS 1. Peripheral neuropathy grade 2 at this assessment (improved from grade 3 with duloxetine and dose reduction). Repeat echocardiogram: LVEF 58% (down from 62% baseline; mild decline, no clinical heart failure). Cardiology notified; surveillance echocardiogram planned post-operatively. Surgery scheduling underway for left modified radical mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy. Cancer present (likely residual), not fully resolved. Patient counseled regarding germline BRCA1 status and option for contralateral prophylactic mastectomy (CPM), which she elected to undergo simultaneously.

At age 58 years (approximately month 7 post-diagnosis), the patient underwent LEFT MODIFIED RADICAL MASTECTOMY with immediate tissue expander placement for reconstruction (left side), CONTRALATERAL PROPHYLACTIC RIGHT MASTECTOMY with immediate tissue expander placement (right side), and SENTINEL LYMPH NODE BIOPSY of the left axilla (3 sentinel nodes identified). The procedure was performed under general anesthesia without intraoperative complications. Estimated blood loss: 280 mL. No intraoperative blood transfusion required. Wound closed primarily. A Jackson-Pratt drain was placed bilaterally. Immediate post-operative period was uneventful. Pathology specimen sent for full analysis.

At age 58 years (surgical specimen, post-neoadjuvant therapy), the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY (left modified radical mastectomy specimen and sentinel lymph nodes). Findings consistent with MIXED PATHOLOGIC RESPONSE (NOT a complete pathologic response; residual invasive disease present). Primary tumor bed: Residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, measuring 1.8 x 1.4 cm (largest dimension). Extensive treatment effect present with fibrosis, foamy macrophages, and hemosiderin deposition occupying approximately 60% of tumor bed area. Residual viable tumor cells present in approximately 40% of tumor bed. Lymphovascular invasion: Present (focal). Margins: All surgical margins negative (closest margin 5 mm). In-situ component: Residual high-grade DCIS present, 3 cm extent (ductal, cribriform pattern). Skin: No dermal involvement. Nipple: No Paget disease. Sentinel lymph nodes: 1 of 3 sentinel lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma (micro-metastasis, 2.1 mm deposit, no extranodal extension). Right breast (CPM): No evidence of malignancy; fibrocystic changes only. Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) calculation: RCB index 2.4, classified as RCB class II (moderate residual disease). Immunohistochemistry on residual tumor: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 negative (confirms triple-negative phenotype maintained post-treatment). Ki-67 on residual tumor: 55% (decreased from pre-treatment 92%, indicating partial treatment effect). Pathologic stage: ypT1cN1mi (AJCC 8th edition), Stage IIA post-neoadjuvant.

At age 58 years (post-operative visit, approximately 3 weeks after surgery), the patient had an oncologist post-operative office assessment. Surgical wounds healing well bilaterally. Tissue expanders in place with planned exchange to permanent implants in approximately 6 months. Drain outputs minimal and drains removed. Pathology results reviewed with patient: residual invasive carcinoma (RCB-II), one positive sentinel lymph node (micro-metastasis). Discussed implications of residual disease and plan for adjuvant therapy per standard of care (CREATE-X/KEYNOTE-522 based: adjuvant capecitabine and pembrolizumab continuation). Given germline BRCA1 mutation, discussed option of adjuvant olaparib (OlympiA trial data) - patient elected to forego olaparib at this time given peripheral neuropathy concerns and polypharmacy burden; option to be revisited. Referral to radiation oncology for post-mastectomy radiation therapy (PMRT) given positive lymph node. ECOG PS 1. Cancer: residual disease, not in complete remission. Follow-up imaging and labs ordered.

At age 58 years (beginning approximately 6 weeks post-surgery, after wound healing confirmed), the patient received POST-MASTECTOMY RADIATION THERAPY (PMRT) to the left chest wall and regional lymphatics (left axilla levels I-III, supraclavicular fossa, internal mammary nodes). Technique: 3D-conformal radiation therapy with tangential fields and supraclavicular field. Total dose: 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) to the chest wall and regional nodes, with a boost of 10 Gy in 5 fractions to the prior tumor bed (total dose to tumor bed: 60.4 Gy). Concurrent pembrolizumab was held during radiation per institutional protocol (last pembrolizumab dose given 3 weeks prior to radiation start). Radiation completed over approximately 7 weeks. No significant acute toxicities during radiation; grade 1 skin erythema at radiation fields, managed with topical aquaphor. No pneumonitis during or immediately after radiation.

At age 58 years (approximately 3 weeks after completion of PMRT), the patient developed grade 2 radiation pneumonitis (new dry cough, mild dyspnea on exertion, low-grade fever 37.9°C). CT chest showed new ground-glass opacities in the left lung, predominantly in the radiation field. Pulmonology consulted. Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (60 mg/day) initiated with planned taper over 8 weeks. Pembrolizumab restart was deferred pending resolution of pneumonitis. The patient also had grade 1-2 left breast/chest wall skin desquamation (moist at folds, managed with wound care). Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine (TSH 2.8 mU/L at this visit). Peripheral neuropathy improved to grade 1 (residual mild tingling in fingertips only).

At age 58 years (approximately 4.5 months post-surgery, after pneumonitis resolved and steroids fully tapered), the patient initiated ADJUVANT CAPECITABINE 1000 mg/m2 orally twice daily on days 1-14 of a 21-day cycle (planned for 8 cycles, per CREATE-X protocol), along with PEMBROLIZUMAB 200 mg IV every 3 weeks (resuming adjuvant phase, per KEYNOTE-522 protocol, for approximately 9 remaining cycles to complete up to 1 year of total pembrolizumab). Patient counseled regarding hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, and mucositis risks with capecitabine. Baseline labs: CBC, CMP, renal function (CrCl 49 mL/min by CKD-EPI; capecitabine dose adjusted to 850 mg/m2 BID given CrCl < 51 mL/min to reduce renal toxicity risk). Thyroid function: normal on levothyroxine. HbA1c: 7.6% (improved with endocrinology co-management).

At age 58 years (cycle 2 of adjuvant capecitabine/pembrolizumab), the patient developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): erythema, peeling, and painful swelling of palms and soles, affecting daily activities. Capecitabine dose held for 7 days, then resumed at 750 mg/m2 BID (further 12% dose reduction from already-adjusted dose). Urea 40% cream applied topically three times daily. Grade 1 mucositis also present (mild oral soreness, no ulcerations). No grade 3/4 toxicities. Pembrolizumab continued at same dose. TSH rechecked: 4.1 mU/L (normal range). No new immune-related adverse events.

At age 59 years (approximately 6 months after surgery, during adjuvant therapy - cycle 4 of capecitabine/pembrolizumab), the patient had a SURVEILLANCE CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST, which showed: No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Lungs: Resolution of prior ground-glass opacities (radiation pneumonitis resolved). No new pulmonary nodules or masses. Chest wall: Post-surgical/post-radiation changes in left chest wall without evidence of local recurrence. No suspicious mediastinal or axillary lymphadenopathy. Liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands: No suspicious lesions. No new findings. Cancer status: No evidence of disease (NED) on imaging. Responding to adjuvant therapy / no active disease identified.

At age 59 years (mid-adjuvant therapy, cycle 5 of capecitabine/pembrolizumab), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Patient tolerating reduced-dose capecitabine reasonably well; hand-foot syndrome improved to grade 1 with dose reduction and topical care. She completed 4 cycles of capecitabine. Pembrolizumab being administered concurrently. Surveillance CT NED. ECOG PS 1. Peripheral neuropathy remains grade 1 (stable, not worsening). Weight stable. Blood pressure well-controlled (132/80 mmHg on lisinopril). Glycemic control improved (HbA1c 7.2%). Patient reporting significant fatigue (grade 2, likely multifactorial: chemotherapy, hypothyroidism, diabetes, emotional distress) and moderate depression/anxiety related to diagnosis and treatment burden; referral to oncology social work and psychiatry initiated. Levothyroxine dose adjusted upward to 88 mcg based on TSH 5.6 mU/L. Cancer: no evidence of disease on imaging. Continues adjuvant therapy.

At age 59 years (approximately 14 months post-surgery, after completing adjuvant capecitabine and during final phase of pembrolizumab maintenance), the patient had a SURVEILLANCE CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST AND BONE SCAN, which showed: CT findings - New 1.4 x 1.2 cm hypoattenuating lesion in the right hepatic lobe (segment VI), new compared to prior imaging. A second smaller 0.8 cm lesion in segment VII. No lymphadenopathy. No pulmonary lesions. Bone scan - New focal area of increased uptake in the right 8th rib posterolaterally, not identified on CT (lytic lesion on targeted CT rib series: 1.5 cm lytic lesion confirmed). Bilateral knee degenerative changes unchanged. Cancer status: SUSPICIOUS FOR METASTATIC DISEASE - new hepatic lesions and right rib lytic lesion consistent with distant metastatic recurrence. Urgent clinical correlation and tissue confirmation recommended.

At age 59 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY (CT-guided core needle biopsy, right hepatic lobe lesion, segment VI). The specimen showed METASTATIC CARCINOMA consistent with breast primary. Tumor cells arranged in nests and cords with central necrosis. Nuclei markedly pleomorphic with prominent nucleoli. Immunohistochemistry: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 negative by IHC (1+), confirming triple-negative phenotype maintained at metastatic site. GATA3 positive (supports breast origin). CK7 positive, CK20 negative, TTF-1 negative, CDX2 negative (excludes lung and GI primary). Ki-67 78%. Diagnosis: Metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma, consistent with known primary. HER2 FISH not repeated at metastatic site at this time (IHC clearly negative).

At age 59 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for metastatic triple-negative breast cancer recurrence based on a specimen obtained from the right hepatic lobe metastasis (core needle biopsy). FoundationOne CDx 500-gene panel. Comparison to prior primary tumor NGS performed. Clinically actionable alterations (new or maintained): TP53 p.R248W (maintained, VAF 71%); BRCA1 biallelic inactivation maintained (germline c.5266dupC + somatic LOH); PIK3CA wild-type (unchanged); PTEN homozygous deletion (maintained); AKT1 p.E17K (NEW somatic mutation, VAF 28%, not present in primary tumor - potential acquired resistance mechanism); MYC amplification (maintained, copy number 22.1, further amplified); CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion (maintained); RB1 loss (maintained). New alterations compared to primary tumor: AKT1 p.E17K (acquired); CCND1 amplification (11q13, copy number 8.2, new - potential acquired resistance); RAD51C p.P34R (VUS, possible role in BRCA pathway). Loss of alterations: ARID1A p.Q330* not detected in metastatic specimen (possible clonal evolution). Fusions: No clinically significant fusions. MSS maintained. TMB: 11 mut/Mb (slight increase). HRD score: 68 (maintained high). Mutational signatures: SBS3 dominant (HRD) with new contribution of SBS17 (possible 5-FU exposure from capecitabine). No NTRK fusions. No FGFR amplification. No KRAS/NRAS mutations.

At age 59 years (upon confirmed diagnosis of metastatic TNBC recurrence, approximately 16 months post-surgery), pembrolizumab maintenance was discontinued. The patient was enrolled in a clinical trial for metastatic TNBC (BRCA1-mutated, platinum-naive). The patient received first-line metastatic TNBC therapy with SACITUZUMAB GOVITECAN-HZIY 10 mg/kg IV on days 1 and 8 of a 21-day cycle (Trodelvy, approved for unresectable or metastatic TNBC after ≥2 prior therapies; used here in context of clinical trial/expanded access as initial metastatic treatment given prior anthracycline, taxane, and capecitabine exposure) PLUS OLAPARIB 300 mg orally twice daily (given germline BRCA1 mutation and HRD-high status; per OlympiAD data and clinical trial combination). Note: physician elected combination given exhaustion of standard agents in neoadjuvant/adjuvant setting and BRCA1 mutation. UGT1A1 genotyping performed: patient is UGT1A1*28/*28 homozygous (increased risk of neutropenia and irinotecan/SN-38 toxicity); sacituzumab govitecan starting dose reduced to 7.5 mg/kg per label guidance for UGT1A1*28 homozygotes.

At age 59 years (cycle 2 of sacituzumab govitecan/olaparib for metastatic disease), the patient developed grade 4 neutropenia (ANC 0.1 x10^9/L) consistent with expected UGT1A1*28/*28-related severe toxicity. She was hospitalized for febrile neutropenia (temperature 38.8°C, ANC 0.08 x10^9/L) and treated with IV piperacillin-tazobactam and filgrastim G-CSF. Blood cultures: negative. CXR: no pneumonia. Duration of hospitalization: 4 days. Sacituzumab govitecan held for this cycle; dose subsequently reduced to 5 mg/kg at next cycle per protocol. Olaparib continued without interruption during hospitalization. Grade 1 diarrhea also present (2-3 loose stools per day), managed with loperamide. Grade 2 fatigue persisted. No new nausea/vomiting. Anemia grade 2 (Hgb 9.1 g/dL); erythropoiesis-stimulating agent not used per institutional policy in metastatic setting; transfusion threshold set at Hgb < 7.5 g/dL.

At age 59 years (after 3 cycles of sacituzumab govitecan/olaparib, approximately 3 months into first-line metastatic treatment), the patient had a RESTAGING CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST, which showed: Right hepatic lesions: segment VI lesion decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.7 cm; segment VII lesion decreased from 0.8 cm to below threshold of reliable measurement (possible complete response at segment VII). No new hepatic lesions. No new pulmonary lesions or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Right 8th rib lytic lesion: stable in size (1.5 cm); no new osseous lesions. No new sites of metastatic disease. Impression: PARTIAL RADIOGRAPHIC RESPONSE to sacituzumab govitecan plus olaparib per RECIST 1.1 (target lesion sum decrease >30%). Cancer status: Present, partial response to first-line metastatic therapy. Responding to therapy.

At age 59 years (clinic visit after restaging imaging, approximately 4 months into metastatic treatment), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Partial response confirmed on CT imaging. Patient tolerated reduced-dose sacituzumab govitecan (5 mg/kg) with improved but persistent grade 1-2 neutropenia (ANC nadir 1.0-1.4 x10^9/L with prophylactic pegfilgrastim given on day 2 of each cycle). Olaparib ongoing at 300 mg BID with manageable nausea (grade 1-2). Fatigue grade 2 persists; functional status ECOG PS 1-2. Pain from right rib lesion managed with acetaminophen/ibuprofen (scheduled); patient declining opioids at this time. Quality of life discussion held; palliative care consultation initiated proactively for symptom management and goals-of-care planning. Patient expressed desire to continue active treatment with curative intent if possible; realistic discussion regarding metastatic disease prognosis held. Peripheral neuropathy grade 1 (stable from prior therapy). Hypothyroidism stable. Glycemic control HbA1c 7.8%. Cancer status: present, responding to first-line metastatic therapy. Overall disease course: metastatic recurrence approximately 14 months after surgery; on active treatment with partial response.

scenario_blurb: Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) stage IIB in a 45-year-old woman, diagnosed after palpable mass and core needle biopsy showing grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma, ER/PR/HER2 negative, Ki-67 85%. Treated with neoadjuvant pembrolizumab plus dose-dense AC followed by paclitaxel, then surgery. Mixed pathologic response with residual disease, followed by adjuvant capecitabine and pembrolizumab maintenance.
